q:
What is the reaction distance when you are driving at 90 km/h?

A. 17 metres.
B. 22 metres.
C. 27 metres.
e:
LESSON 13
Between the moment the driver sees the danger and the moment the brain processes this information and realises that the brake pedal must be pressed, the vehicle has already travelled a certain distance.
The higher the speed, the longer the distance covered during the reaction time.
• At 50 km/h, the reaction distance is about 15 metres.
• At 90 km/h, the reaction distance is about 27 metres.
• At 120 km/h, the reaction distance is about 36 metres.
- This happens before the brake pedal is pressed.
- A drunk driver reacts more slowly, which makes the reaction distance even longer.

q:
You drive 120 km/hour on this motorway.
The stopping distance is about …

A. 60 meters.
B. 80 meters.
C. 110 meters.
e:
LESSON 13 – ‘The stopping distance’.
50 km/hour: 30 meters.
100 km/hour: 80 meters.
120 km/hour: 110 meters.

q:
You have to slow down.
Is it correct that the faster you drive, the longer the stopping distance will be?

B. False.
e:
LESSON 13 – ‘The stopping distance’.
At 50 km/hour is the thinking or reaction distance for example 15 meters,
At 90 km/hour 27 meters.
At 120 km/hour 36 meters (before you have pressed the brake pedal).

q:
Is the braking distance shorter or longer when driving on snow?

A. Shorter.
B. Longer.
C. Makes no difference.
e: When driving on snow, the braking distance can be up to four times longer.
clean_q:
Is the braking distance shorter or longer when driving on snow?
A. Shorter.
B. Longer.
C. Makes no difference.
clean_e: When driving on snow, the braking distance can be up to four times longer.

q:
When you brake with ABS on a dry road surface, the stopping distance is shorter than without ABS on a dry road surface.

A. Correct.
B. False.
e:
LESSON 13 – ‘The stopping distance’.
Braking with ABS doesn’t necessarily mean that the stopping distance is shorter, than braking without ABS.

LESSON 13 – ‘The stopping distance’.

q:
Another word for the braking distance is the stopping distance.

A. Correct.
B. False.
e:
LESSON 13 – ‘The stopping distance’.
The braking distance is the distance the car travels, starting from the moment when the driver presses the brake pedal, to the car completely stationary.

A. Correct.
B. False.

LESSON 13 – ‘The stopping distance’.
The ABS-system makes sure that the driver is always in control of the car.

q:
You are driving at 120 km/h on a motorway and it starts raining heavily.
What do you do?

A. Keep a safe distance of 60 metres.
B. Slow down and increase the distance.
e:
LESSON 13
The faster you drive, the more distance you must keep.
A simple formula helps you calculate it: speed divided by two.
At 120 km/h, the safe following distance is 60 metres.
At 100 km/h, it becomes 50 metres.
But in heavy rain, snow or fog, you must slow down and increase the following distance.

A. 45 meters.
B. 55 meters.
C. 65 meters.

LESSON 13 – ‘The stopping distance’.
Via a simple formula, you can calculate that distance: the speed at which you drive, divided by two.

q:
A driver has to make a long trip abroad.
How often should he ideally take a break?

A. Every half hour.
B. Every hour.
C. Every two hours.
e:
LESSON 13
During a long car journey, it is recommended to take a short break of at least 15 minutes about every two hours to prevent fatigue and stay alert. Make sure to get out of the car, stretch your legs, take deep breaths, and stay hydrated with water or light snacks.
